docs.title: AI Sales Manager — Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the maintained documentation for AI Sales Manager: an autonomous B2B outbound sales assistant using real Telegram accounts and LLM-driven dialogue.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Product Roadmap
  • Testing Strategy
  • Quality Requirements
  • Quality Requirement Tests
  • User Acceptance Tests
  • Definition of Done
  • Interface Specification
  • User Story Index
  • Architecture (maintained by the architecture team)
  • Development Process (maintained by the process team)

docs.title: Repository
docs.text:
• Source code: app/
  • CI status: GitHub Actions
  • Latest release: Releases
  • Week 5 report: reports/week5/README.md

docs.title: What is AI Sales Manager?
docs.text: The system automates B2B outbound sales over Telegram using live MTProto accounts and large language models. It sends first messages, handles replies, manages conversation state, books meetings, and alerts operators about hot leads.

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
• MVP v1 delivered and approved.
  • MVP v2 delivered as SemVer release v0.3.0 (Assignment 5 Sprint increment).

docs.title: Definition of Done — AI Sales Manager
docs.text: A Product Backlog Item (PBI) may only be marked Done when every item in this checklist is satisfied. This Definition of Done applies to all Sprint work from Assignment 4 onward.

docs.title: 1. Acceptance Criteria Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] All acceptance criteria defined on the issue are met and verified by the implementer
  • [ ] Edge cases described in the acceptance criteria have been manually tested or covered by automated tests

docs.title: 2. Review by Another Team Member
docs.text:
• [ ] The PR/MR has been reviewed and approved by at least one team member who is not the implementer
  • [ ] A named backup reviewer is available if the primary reviewer is blocked for more than 24 hours
  • [ ] All reviewer comments are resolved before merge

docs.title: 3. Passing CI Checks
docs.text:
• [ ] The test CI job passes — all pytest tests green with no regressions
  • [ ] The security CI job passes — Bandit reports no new medium or high severity issues (bandit -r app/ -ll)
  • [ ] The pip-audit CI job passes — no known vulnerabilities in dependencies
  • [ ] The lint CI job passes — flake8 reports no errors (flake8 app/ --max-line-length=120)
  • [ ] The link-check CI job passes — no broken links in Markdown files (lychee)
  • [ ] CI runs on the protected default branch (main) and shows a green status before merge

docs.title: 4. Relevant Automated Tests
docs.text:
• [ ] New logic is covered by at least one automated unit or integration test
  • [ ] All existing tests continue to pass after the change
  • [ ] Tests are stored in tests/ following the existing naming convention (test_<module>.py)
  • [ ] Tests are linked from docs/testing.md if they cover a new module or component

docs.title: 5. Relevant Automated Quality Requirement Tests
docs.text:
• [ ] If the change affects app/core/state_machine.py → tests/test_core_state_machine.py still passes (QRT-02)
  • [ ] If the change affects app/llm/guardrails.py → tests/test_llm_guardrails.py still passes (QRT-01, QRT-04)
  • [ ] If the change affects app/core/scheduler.py → tests/test_core_scheduler.py still passes (QRT-03)
  • [ ] If the change introduces a new quality requirement → a linked QRT is defined in docs/quality-requirement-tests.md
  • [ ] If the change introduces or affects an architecture decision → the related ADR in docs/architecture/adr/ is updated or created

docs.title: 6. Coverage Expectations for Critical Modules
docs.text:
• [ ] Each critical module maintains at least 30% line coverage after the change:
Module Minimum coverage app/core/state_machine.py 30% (currently ~100%) app/llm/engine.py 30% (currently ~99%) app/llm/guardrails.py 30% (currently ~98%) app/services/notification_service.py 30% (currently ~96%) app/core/scheduler.py 30% (currently ~80%) app/bots/inbound_listener.py 30% (currently ~75%) app/services/conversation_service.py 30% (currently ~70%) app/core/humanizer.py 30% (currently ~68%)
  • [ ] Coverage report is visible in CI artifacts or PR/MR comments

docs.title: 7. Testing Evidence Preserved
docs.text:
• [ ] CI run link is attached to the PR/MR or issue before closing
  • [ ] Coverage report or screenshot is included in the PR/MR description or linked from the CI run
  • [ ] If a QRT was added or modified, the evidence is linked from docs/quality-requirement-tests.md

docs.title: 8. Changelog Update for User-Visible Changes
docs.text:
• [ ] If the change is user-visible (new feature, bug fix, behavior change), an entry is added to CHANGELOG.md under [Unreleased]
  • [ ] Format follows Keep a Changelog: ### Added, ### Fixed, ### Changed, ### Removed
  • [ ] Internal refactors, test additions, and documentation-only changes do not require a changelog entry

docs.title: 9. Release and Deployment Evidence (for MVP v2 increments)
docs.text:
• [ ] The change is merged into the protected default branch (main) through an issue-linked PR/MR
  • [ ] A SemVer release (v0.3.0) is created for the MVP v2 increment and links to:
  • Sprint 3 milestone
  • Deployment or run instructions
  • Public sanitized demo video
  • Week 5 public report (reports/week5/README.md)
  • [ ] The current product increment remains deployed and accessible to the customer and TA until grading is complete
  • [ ] The Week 5 public report is updated with relevant links, screenshots, and status

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text:
If later project work changes any of the following, this file must be updated in the same PR/MR:

  • Product stack (new language, framework, or runtime)
  • Quality requirements (docs/quality-requirements.md)
  • Critical modules list or coverage thresholds
  • CI configuration (.github/workflows/ci.yml)
  • QRT files (docs/quality-requirement-tests.md)

Do not leave Assignment 4 gates stale when the product evolves.

docs.title: Development Process and Configuration Management
docs.text: This document describes how the AI Sales Manager team develops, reviews, and releases the product.

docs.title: Git Workflow
docs.text: (Insert Mermaid gitGraph diagram here and explain the workflow.)

docs.title: Definition of Done
docs.text: See docs/definition-of-done.md.

docs.title: Configuration Management
docs.text:
• Environment variables are documented in .env.example.
  • Secrets and credentials are never committed to the repository.
  • CI/CD configuration lives in .github/workflows/.

docs.title: Sprint Workflow
docs.text:
• Sprint Planning produces a Sprint Goal and selected PBIs.
  • Work is tracked through GitHub issues and milestones.
  • Changes are made via issue-linked Pull Requests reviewed by another team member.
  • Releases use Semantic Versioning and are created from the protected main branch.

This file is a placeholder maintained by the process team. Replace with full development-process content for Assignment 5 Part 3.

docs.title: Interface Type
docs.text: Web application (responsive, desktop-first) with a complementary Telegram bot.

docs.title: Intended Users
docs.text:
• Sales Director — primary user; manages AI managers, contacts, tasks, and reviews analytics.
  • Operator — secondary user; monitors dialogs and manually qualifies leads.

docs.title: 1. Login / Authentication
docs.text:
• Email/password or Telegram-based auth.
  • Role-based redirect: Sales Director → Dashboard, Operator → Dialogs.

docs.title: 2. Dashboard (Home)
docs.text:
• Summary stats: total contacts, active tasks, qualified leads, conversion rate.
  • Quick-action buttons: "New AI Manager", "New Task", "Import Contacts".
  • Recent activity feed.

docs.title: 3a. Contact List
docs.text:
• Table with columns: Name, Telegram ID / Phone, Position, Company, Status (cold/warm/hot), Date Added.
  • Search bar + filter by status, company, date range.
  • Bulk select → "Add to Task".

docs.title: 3b. Import Contacts
docs.text:
• Upload CSV/Excel file (drag-and-drop or file picker).
  • Preview parsed data before importing.
  • Map columns: name, telegram_id, phone, position, company.
  • Success/error feedback with count.

docs.title: 3c. Contact Parsing (US-09)
docs.text:
• Form fields: Industry, Position, Geography.
  • "Start Parsing" button with progress indicator.
  • Results preview before saving to contact base.

docs.title: 4a. AI Manager List
docs.text:
• Cards or table showing: Name, Role, Status (active/inactive), Linked Task count.
  • "Create New" button (max 5 limit indicator).
  • Duplicate button (US-11).

docs.title: 4b. Create / Edit AI Manager
docs.text:
• Name field.
  • Role / Persona textarea: describe the AI's role, tone, and style.
  • Target Audience textarea: describe the ideal lead profile.
  • Dialogue Goal textarea: what the AI should achieve (e.g., book a call).
  • Success Criterion textarea: what constitutes a successful conversation.
  • LLM Provider dropdown: Qwen, Gemini, DeepSeek (US-13).
  • Save / Cancel buttons.

docs.title: 5a. Task List
docs.text:
• Table: Task Name, AI Manager, Contact Base, Status (running/paused/stopped), Progress (X/Y processed).
  • Start / Pause / Stop buttons per task.

docs.title: 5b. Create Task
docs.text:
• Step 1: Select AI Manager (from existing list).
  • Step 2: Select Contact Base (from imported/parsed contacts).
  • Step 3: Configure Working Hours (checkbox + time picker, default 09:00–18:00 MSK).
  • Step 4: Configure Message Interval (initial message + follow-up after N hours).
  • Step 5: Review & Launch.

docs.title: 6. Dialog Monitor (/dialogs)
docs.text:
• List of all active/inactive dialogs.
  • Each row: Contact Name, AI Manager, Last Message, Status (cold/warm/hot/qualified/rejected).
  • Click to expand → full message history with timestamps.
  • Operator action buttons: "Mark Qualified", "Mark Rejected" (US-07).
  • Tags on ambiguous replies (US-12).
  • Search by contact name or status filter.

docs.title: 7a. Funnel Visualization
docs.text:
• 3–4 stage funnel: Cold → Warm → Hot → Qualified.
  • Count at each stage with conversion rate.
  • Historical trend (line chart) for each stage over time.

docs.title: 7b. Reports
docs.text:
• Contacts Found (total parsed/imported).
  • Contacts Contacted (messaged by AI).
  • Moved to Next Stage (per stage).
  • Qualified / Rejected breakdown.

docs.title: 8. Settings (/settings)
docs.text:
• Working hours configuration (default 09:00–18:00 MSK).
  • Message interval settings.
  • LLM API key configuration.
  • Notification preferences (Telegram bot alerts).

docs.title: Flow A: Import Contacts → Create AI Manager → Launch Task
docs.text:
1. Upload CSV at /contacts/import → preview → confirm.
  2. Create AI manager at /ai-managers/new → fill persona details → save.
  3. Create task at /tasks/new → select AI manager + contact base → configure hours → launch.
  4. Monitor progress on Dashboard and /dialogs.

docs.title: Flow B: Daily Review
docs.text:
1. Check Dashboard for summary stats.
  2. Review /analytics funnel stages.
  3. Open /dialogs to manually qualify leads.
  4. Export qualified leads or flag for follow-up.

docs.title: Empty States
docs.text:
• No contacts yet → "Import your first contact base to get started" with CTA.
  • No AI managers yet → "Create your first AI manager" with CTA.
  • No tasks yet → "Launch your first task" with CTA.
  • No dialogs yet → "Dialogs will appear once tasks are running."

docs.title: Loading States
docs.text:
• Skeleton placeholders on table and card loads.
  • Progress bar during CSV parsing and task execution.

docs.title: Error States
docs.text:
• CSV parse error → inline error message with row details.
  • Task failure → status badge (red) + error detail tooltip.
  • API timeout → retry button with message.

docs.title: Success States
docs.text:
• Contact import → toast: "150 contacts imported successfully".
  • Task launched → toast + redirect to task detail.
  • Lead qualified → inline confirmation + status update.

docs.title: Telegram Bot (Secondary Interface — US-10)
docs.text:
• Commands:
  • /start — register for notifications.
  • /tasks — list active tasks.
  • /stats — quick funnel summary.
  • /help — list available commands.

docs.title: Implementation Status for MVP v0
docs.text: Screen Implemented Mocked Planned Login — Mock MVP v1 Dashboard — Mock MVP v1 Contact Import Partial (CSV API exists) — MVP v0 Contact Parsing — — V2 AI Manager Studio — — MVP v1 Task Management — — MVP v1 Dialog Monitor — — MVP v1 Funnel & Analytics — — MVP v1 Settings — — MVP v1 Telegram Bot Partial (bot exists) — MVP v0

docs.title: Quality Requirement Tests (QRTs)
docs.text:
This document maps each quality requirement defined in docs/quality-requirements.md to one or more automated quality requirement tests.

An automated QRT is a test that runs in CI, produces deterministic pass/fail output, and is stored in the normal repository test location (tests/).

docs.title: QRT-01: LLM Response Safety
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-01
  • Related ADR: ADR-001 — LLM Output Guardrails
  • Verification method: Automated unit tests for app/llm/guardrails.py.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment with fixture inputs containing forbidden topics, markdown, CJK/Arabic characters, and bot self-identification.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_llm_guardrails.py -v
  • Expected measurable result: 100% of guardrail-violating inputs are rejected; valid inputs pass.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: QRT-02: Conversation State Correctness
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-02
  • Related ADR: ADR-002 — Deterministic Conversation State Machine
  • Verification method: Automated unit tests for app/core/state_machine.py.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment covering all defined states, events, terminal states, and unknown inputs.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_core_state_machine.py -v
  • Expected measurable result: All state transitions are correct; terminal states are immutable; 100% branch coverage.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: QRT-03: Outbound Message Delivery Performance
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-03
  • Related ADR: ADR-003 — Scheduler-Driven Outbound Processing
  • Verification method: Automated integration tests for app/core/scheduler.py with mocked LLM and Pyrogram.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment with a campaign containing multiple pending contacts and available accounts.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_core_scheduler.py -v
  • Expected measurable result: All pending contacts are processed without timeout; account selection logic completes in < 1 ms per contact in unit tests.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: QRT-04: Anti-Repetition in Generated Messages
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-04
  • Related ADR: ADR-004 — Anti-Repetition Check for Generated Messages
  • Verification method: Automated unit tests for app/llm/guardrails.py anti-repetition logic.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment with messages at and around the 0.8 similarity threshold.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_llm_guardrails.py::test_check_anti_repetition -v
  • Expected measurable result: Similar messages (≥ 0.8) are rejected; distinct messages pass.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: QRT-05: Prompt Configuration Maintainability
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-05
  • Related ADR: ADR-005 — External Prompt Configuration and Versioning
  • Verification method: Unit tests for app.config.prompts.load_prompt_config() and prompt builders.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment with the default app/config/prompts/v1.json.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_llm_prompts.py tests/test_llm_funnel_prompts.py -v
  • Expected measurable result: Prompt config loads without errors; system/reply/follow-up prompts render expected stage-specific content.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: QRT-06: Funnel Definition Validity
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-06
  • Related ADR: ADR-006 — Funnel Upload and Preview API
  • Verification method: Integration tests for POST /api/funnels/preview and POST /api/funnels/upload.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment with valid/invalid JSON and plain-text funnel payloads.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_api_funnels.py -v
  • Expected measurable result: Invalid funnels return HTTP 422; valid funnels are accepted with normalized stages.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: QRT-07: Production Health Observability
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-07
  • Related ADR: ADR-007 — Production Monitoring and Logging
  • Verification method: Integration test for GET /health.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment with mocked database and scheduler.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_api_health.py -v
  • Expected measurable result: Endpoint returns status, db, and scheduler fields within 500 ms; degraded state reported when DB or scheduler is down.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: QRT-08: AI Automation Rate Accuracy
docs.text:
• Linked quality requirement: QR-08
  • Related ADR: ADR-008 — AI-Automation Rate Tracking
  • Verification method: Integration tests for GET /analytics/automation-rate and conversation status updates.
  • Test data, setup, or environment: Standard pytest environment with mocked Conversation counts and status-change scenarios.
  • Automated command or CI check: pytest tests/test_api_analytics.py tests/test_api_conversations.py -v
  • Expected measurable result: Rate endpoint returns correct ai_handled, escalated, and rate_pct; status updates mark conversations as escalated.
  • Evidence location: Latest protected-default-branch CI run.

docs.title: Running the QRTs
docs.text:
# QRTs only (run through their respective test modules)
pytest tests/test_llm_guardrails.py tests/test_core_state_machine.py tests/test_core_scheduler.py tests/test_llm_prompts.py tests/test_api_funnels.py tests/test_api_health.py tests/test_api_analytics.py tests/test_api_conversations.py -v

# Full test suite including QRTs
pytest tests/ -v --cov=app --cov-report=term-missing

The QRTs are included in the default test command used by CI.

docs.title: Quality Requirements — AI Sales Manager
docs.text:
This document defines quality requirements using ISO/IEC 25010 quality sub-characteristics. Each requirement uses the measurable scenario format: stimulus → response → measurable outcome.

Each relevant quality requirement links to the architecture decision(s) that address it (see docs/architecture/adr/) and to automated quality requirement tests in docs/quality-requirement-tests.md.

ID Sub-characteristic Related ADR QR-01 Confidentiality (Security) ADR-001 QR-02 Fault Tolerance (Reliability) ADR-002 QR-03 Time Behaviour (Performance Efficiency) ADR-003 QR-04 User Error Protection (Usability) ADR-004

docs.title: QR-01: LLM Response Safety (Security — Confidentiality)
docs.text: ID: QR-01 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: Confidentiality (Security) Rationale: The system generates outbound messages on behalf of real Telegram accounts. A message that contains forbidden topics (politics, religion, hate speech, bot self-identification) can damage the client's brand, expose legal liability, or trigger Telegram account bans. Guardrails must block such content before delivery.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus LLM returns a response containing a forbidden topic keyword, a markdown symbol, CJK/Arabic characters, or the word "бот" (bot) Response apply_guardrails() returns None; the message is not sent; the system retries generation Measurable outcome 100% of guardrail-violating messages are blocked in automated tests; no forbidden content reaches SellerClient.send_message()

Linked tests: tests/test_llm_guardrails.py Related ADR: ADR-001 — LLM Output Guardrails

docs.title: QR-02: Conversation State Correctness (Reliability — Fault Tolerance)
docs.text: ID: QR-02 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: Fault Tolerance (Reliability) Rationale: The sales funnel depends on deterministic state transitions. An incorrect transition (e.g., moving a meeting_booked lead back to warm) corrupts the funnel, sends messages to closed leads, and inflates analytics. The state machine must produce correct outputs for all defined inputs and must be immutable — terminal states must never be exited.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus Any event is applied to any conversation state, including terminal states (meeting_booked, closed) and unknown states Response transition(state, event) returns the correct next state; terminal states return themselves for any event; unknown states/events return the current state unchanged Measurable outcome 100% branch coverage on app/core/state_machine.py; all 22 test cases in test_core_state_machine.py pass

Linked tests: tests/test_core_state_machine.py Related ADR: ADR-002 — Deterministic Conversation State Machine

docs.title: QR-03: Outbound Message Delivery Performance (Performance Efficiency — Time Behaviour)
docs.text: ID: QR-03 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: Time Behaviour (Performance Efficiency) Rationale: The system processes campaigns every 5 minutes. If account selection, LLM generation, or message dispatch takes longer than the scheduler interval, jobs stack up, duplicate messages are sent, and daily limits are exceeded. The critical path from scheduler tick to message sent must complete within a bounded time.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus APScheduler fires process_campaigns() for a campaign with 10 pending contacts and 3 available Telegram accounts Response The scheduler selects accounts, generates messages, and dispatches all pending contacts within the cycle Measurable outcome Scheduler unit tests mock LLM and Pyrogram and assert all contacts are processed without timeout; account selection logic completes in < 1 ms per contact in unit tests

Linked tests: tests/test_core_scheduler.py Related ADR: ADR-003 — Scheduler-Driven Outbound Processing

docs.title: QR-04: Anti-Repetition in Generated Messages (Usability — User Error Protection)
docs.text: ID: QR-04 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: User Error Protection (Usability) Rationale: Sending the same or near-identical message twice to the same lead signals automation, damages trust, and may trigger Telegram spam filters. The guardrails must detect Levenshtein similarity ≥ 0.8 with any of the last 5 messages and reject the repeated output.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus LLM generates a message with cosine/Levenshtein similarity ≥ 0.8 to one of the last 5 outbound messages in the same conversation Response check_anti_repetition() returns False; apply_guardrails() returns None; generation is retried Measurable outcome Automated test confirms repetition check blocks similar messages and passes distinct messages; threshold 0.8 is tested with boundary values

Linked tests: tests/test_llm_guardrails.py (test_check_anti_repetition_*) Related ADR: ADR-004 — Anti-Repetition Check for Generated Messages

docs.title: QR-05: Prompt Configuration Maintainability (Maintainability — Modifiability)
docs.text: ID: QR-05 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: Modifiability (Maintainability) Rationale: LLM prompts need frequent tuning based on customer feedback and A/B tests. Embedding prompts in Python code forces a full redeploy for every wording change and mixes prompt content with business logic, making reviews and rollbacks harder.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus Product owner wants to change the system prompt tone, nurturing rules, or intent-classification examples Response The change is made in app/config/prompts/v1.json; prompt builders load the external config at startup Measurable outcome No application code is modified; existing automated tests for prompt rendering still pass; the config version is visible in logs

Linked tests: tests/test_llm_prompts.py, tests/test_llm_funnel_prompts.py, tests/test_core_funnel.py Related ADR: ADR-005 — External Prompt Configuration and Versioning

docs.title: QR-06: Funnel Definition Validity (Functional Suitability — Functional Correctness)
docs.text: ID: QR-06 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: Functional Correctness (Functional Suitability) Rationale: Operators can upload custom sales funnels via API. An invalid funnel (missing stage names, duplicate stages, unsupported format) could break prompt generation or stage progression, leading to incorrect outbound messages.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus Operator uploads a funnel definition in JSON or plain text Response parse_funnel() validates the structure, rejects duplicates, ensures every stage has a name, and returns a normalized stage list Measurable outcome 100% of invalid inputs return HTTP 422 with a clear error; valid funnels are accepted and persisted; automated parser tests cover JSON, text, duplicate, and missing-name cases

Linked tests: tests/test_api_funnels.py Related ADR: ADR-006 — Funnel Upload and Preview API

docs.title: QR-07: Production Health Observability (Reliability — Availability / Maintainability)
docs.text: ID: QR-07 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: Availability (Reliability) / Maintainability Rationale: In production, the team and customer need to know whether the API, scheduler, database, and Redis are healthy. Without a lightweight health endpoint and structured logs, failures are discovered only when messages stop sending.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus Uptime monitor or operator polls service health every 30 seconds Response GET /health checks PostgreSQL and scheduler status and returns ok or degraded; containers restart automatically on failure Measurable outcome Health endpoint responds in < 500 ms; automated health test passes; Docker Compose health check and restart: unless-stopped are configured for all services

Linked tests: tests/test_api_health.py Related ADR: ADR-007 — Production Monitoring and Logging

docs.title: QR-08: AI Automation Rate Accuracy (Functional Suitability — Accuracy)
docs.text: ID: QR-08 ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic: Accuracy (Functional Suitability) Rationale: The team and customer need an objective metric of how many dialogs are handled end-to-end by the AI versus escalated to a human operator. An inaccurate metric would mislead prioritization of automation improvements.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Field Value Stimulus Operator calls GET /analytics/automation-rate Response The endpoint counts total conversations and those with was_escalated = true, then returns ai_handled, escalated, and rate_pct Measurable outcome Computed values match direct DB counts; tests verify zero-total handling and normal-case arithmetic; any operator-driven status change sets was_escalated = true

Linked tests: tests/test_api_analytics.py, tests/test_api_conversations.py Related ADR: ADR-008 — AI-Automation Rate Tracking

docs.title: Product Goal
docs.text: Build an autonomous B2B outbound sales assistant that uses real Telegram accounts and LLM-driven dialogue to generate qualified meetings with minimal human intervention.

docs.title: Sprint 1 — MVP v1
docs.text:
Goal: Deliver a working sales funnel for automated Telegram outreach, from first contact to meeting booking.

Dates: 2026-06-09 – 2026-06-20

Key Deliverables:

  • Configurable 4-stage sales funnel (hook → qualification → value → CTA)
  • Funnel-aware LLM prompt generation
  • Funnel stage tracking in Conversation
  • Admin bot script creation with first_message_goal selection
  • Analytics dashboard for replies, qualified leads, and meetings booked
  • Multi-provider LLM support (OpenRouter + DashScope)

docs.title: Sprint 2 — Campaign Launch and Operational Readiness
docs.text:
Goal: Deliver a usable campaign-launch workflow for AI-powered outreach while improving product reliability and reducing implementation risks through validation, testing, and documentation improvements.

Dates: 2026-06-25 – 2026-07-09

Sprint Focus:

  • Complete campaign-launch functionality
  • Improve data import and campaign execution reliability
  • Reduce operational risks
  • Improve workflow readiness and documentation
  • Maintain Sprint traceability and planning structure

docs.title: Sprint 3 — MVP v2: Production Deployment and AI Conversation Upgrade
docs.text:
Goal: Deliver MVP v2 by deploying the application to a production VPS for reliable 24/7 availability and enhancing the AI assistant with improved prompts, a more natural conversational flow, and a structured lead nurturing process that builds trust before guiding users through the sales funnel.

Dates: 2026-06-29 – 2026-07-04

Sprint Focus:

  • Deploy application to production VPS for 24/7 availability
  • Improve AI prompt quality for lead nurturing
  • Implement natural multi-stage conversation flow
  • Optimize sales funnel progression and user engagement
  • Configure production infrastructure and monitoring
  • Introduce prompt versioning and maintainability improvements

Selected Sprint PBIs:

  • US-06: Increase lead turnover
  • US-07: 24/7 availability
  • US-017: Improve AI Prompt Quality for Lead Nurturing
  • US-018: Implement Natural Multi-Stage Conversation Flow
  • TECH-04: Implement sales script and funnel file upload API
  • TECH-05: Build funnel preview endpoint and stage validation
  • TECH-06: Track AI-automation rate per dialog session
  • TECH-11: Deploy Application to Production VPS
  • TECH-12: Configure Production Infrastructure and Monitoring
  • TECH-13: Prompt Configuration and Versioning

Sprint Scope Rationale:

This Sprint focuses on delivering MVP v2 by deploying the application to a production VPS for reliable 24/7 availability and improving the AI assistant's conversational quality through enhanced prompts, a more natural dialogue flow, and an optimized lead-nurturing process.

The selected scope also includes production infrastructure improvements to increase system reliability, maintainability, and operational readiness while creating a more engaging user experience that supports higher-quality customer interactions and more effective sales conversion.

Story Point Summary:

  • User stories: 23 SP
  • Technical tasks: 12 SP
  • Sprint 3 total: 35 SP

docs.title: Sprint 4 — Campaign Execution and Intelligence Layer
docs.text:
Goal: Enhance campaign execution capabilities and introduce deeper intelligence into lead management and analytics.

Planned PBIs:

  • US-09: Manual Dialog Takeover
  • US-010: Telegram Admin Bot for Management
  • US-013: Monitor Active Dialogs in Real Time
  • US-015: Campaign Analytics and Conversion Dashboard

Planned Outcomes:

  • Better operator control over conversations
  • Improved real-time monitoring of campaigns
  • Stronger analytics and conversion tracking
  • Enhanced operational visibility

docs.title: Future Directions
docs.text:
• Voice message support
  • Image and media processing
  • CRM integrations (HubSpot, Pipedrive)
  • Calendar integrations (Google Calendar, Calendly)
  • Advanced campaign analytics
  • Real-time dashboards
  • A/B testing for outreach campaigns
  • Infrastructure scaling and performance optimization

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The project uses pytest for all automated testing. Tests are organized by module in tests/. The test suite contains 459 tests with approximately 81% line coverage across the application.

Run the full suite:

pytest tests/ -v --cov=app --cov-report=term-missing

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
Unit tests cover isolated business logic with mocked dependencies.

File Module What it tests tests/test_core_state_machine.py app/core/state_machine.py All state transitions, terminal state immutability, unknown event handling tests/test_llm_guardrails.py app/llm/guardrails.py Length check, forbidden topics, anti-repetition, bot-word detection, markdown detection tests/test_core_humanizer.py app/core/humanizer.py Typing delays, message chunking, markdown stripping, casual marker injection tests/test_llm_engine.py app/llm/engine.py LLM cascade fallback, retry logic, hardcoded fallback tests/test_llm_intent_classifier.py app/llm/intent_classifier.py Intent classification outputs tests/test_llm_prompts.py app/llm/prompts.py Prompt template rendering tests/test_llm_funnel_prompts.py app/llm/prompts.py + app/core/funnel.py Stage-aware prompt generation tests/test_core_funnel.py app/core/funnel.py Stage progression, aliases, CTA rules tests/test_core_account_manager.py app/core/account_manager.py Account selection, cooldown logic, daily limit enforcement tests/test_db_redis.py app/db/redis.py Redis cache read/write/invalidation

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
Integration tests verify interactions between components using AsyncMock for external services.

File Components covered tests/test_core_scheduler.py Scheduler ↔ Account Manager ↔ LLM Engine ↔ DB tests/test_bots_inbound_listener.py Inbound Listener ↔ Conversation Service ↔ LLM Engine tests/test_bots_seller_client.py SellerClient ↔ Pyrogram (mocked) tests/test_services_conversation_service.py Conversation Service ↔ DB ↔ Redis tests/test_services_contact_import.py CSV/Excel import ↔ DB tests/test_humanizer_integration.py Humanizer ↔ SellerClient (typing simulation) tests/test_api_campaigns.py FastAPI campaign endpoints ↔ DB tests/test_api_conversations.py FastAPI conversation endpoints ↔ DB tests/test_api_funnels.py FastAPI funnel preview/upload endpoints ↔ parser ↔ DB tests/test_api_analytics.py FastAPI analytics endpoints (automation-rate) ↔ DB

docs.title: End-to-End Tests
docs.text: File What it covers tests/test_e2e.py Full campaign cycle: import → schedule → send → inbound reply → state transition → notification

docs.title: Scenario / Regression Tests
docs.text: File Risk addressed tests/test_flood_wait.py Pyrogram FloodWait handling and account cooldown tests/test_timezone.py Working hours filter with different timezones tests/test_deduplication.py Duplicate contact prevention during import tests/test_auto_close.py 48 h auto-close when lead does not reply tests/test_daily_reset.py Daily message counter reset at 00:00 MSK tests/test_inbound_fallback.py LLM failure fallback to hardcoded Russian message tests/test_validation.py Contact validation (phone/username format) tests/test_facts_extraction.py LLM-extracted facts stored in conversation

docs.title: Critical Modules and Coverage
docs.text:
Module Coverage Threshold Status app/core/state_machine.py ~100% ≥ 30% ✅ app/api/analytics.py ~100% ≥ 30% ✅ app/api/conversations.py ~100% ≥ 30% ✅ app/api/health.py ~100% ≥ 30% ✅ app/llm/engine.py ~94% ≥ 30% ✅ app/llm/guardrails.py ~90% ≥ 30% ✅ app/services/notification_service.py ~94% ≥ 30% ✅ app/core/scheduler.py ~80% ≥ 30% ✅ app/bots/inbound_listener.py ~79% ≥ 30% ✅ app/services/conversation_service.py ~86% ≥ 30% ✅ app/core/humanizer.py ~92% ≥ 30% ✅ app/core/funnel.py ~88% ≥ 30% ✅ app/services/funnel_parser.py ~88% ≥ 30% ✅ app/logging_config.py ~100% ≥ 30% ✅ Global ~81% — ✅

All critical modules exceed the 30% minimum line coverage threshold.

docs.title: Additional QA Check: Static Security Analysis with Bandit
Options Considered
docs.text: Tool Purpose Decision bandit Python security linter — detects hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, unsafe subprocess calls Selected pip-audit Checks dependencies for known CVEs Selected lychee Broken-link checker for Markdown files Selected radon Cyclomatic complexity — flags overly complex functions Deferred (not a security risk) pylint General code quality Not selected — overlaps with existing flake8 linting

docs.title: Selected Check: Bandit Security Analysis
docs.text:
QA objective: Detect Python security anti-patterns introduced during development — hardcoded secrets, use of eval(), unsafe subprocess calls, and insecure hash functions.

Why this matters: The system handles Telegram session strings (sensitive credentials) and executes scheduled tasks. A developer accidentally hardcoding an API key or using eval() on LLM output could expose accounts or allow remote code execution.

Where it runs in CI: Runs as a separate security job in .github/workflows/ci.yml, independent of the test job so a security finding does not block coverage reporting.

bandit -r app/ -ll --format txt

The -ll flag reports only medium and high severity issues to avoid noise from low-severity style warnings.

Important limitations: - Bandit is a static analyzer — it does not detect runtime injection via LLM output. - Some subprocess calls in scripts/ are legitimate; these are suppressed with # nosec comments where reviewed and intentional.

docs.title: Selected Check: Dependency Vulnerability Scan with pip-audit
docs.text:
QA objective: Detect known vulnerabilities in Python dependencies before they reach production.

Why this matters: The product relies on external packages for Telegram access, LLM calls, and web framework functionality. A vulnerable dependency could expose session strings or API keys.

Where it runs in CI: Runs as a separate pip-audit job in .github/workflows/ci.yml.

pip-audit --requirement requirements.txt --desc

docs.title: Selected Check: Broken-Link Check with lychee
docs.text:
QA objective: Keep documentation and report links valid.

Why this matters: Broken links in reports, README, or documentation make the project hard to inspect and reduce trust during grading.

Where it runs in CI: Runs in .github/workflows/links.yml on every push and PR.

lychee .

docs.title: Adapted Testing Strategy for LLM-Heavy Components
docs.text:
The LLM engine (app/llm/engine.py) cannot be tested against real API calls in CI because: - API keys are not available in CI. - LLM outputs are non-deterministic.

Approach: All LLM calls are mocked with AsyncMock returning deterministic fixture responses. Tests verify the orchestration logic (retry, fallback cascade, guardrails integration) rather than LLM output quality. Manual prompt quality review is performed separately during Sprint Reviews.

docs.title: Running Tests Locally
docs.text:
# Full suite with coverage
pytest tests/ -v --cov=app --cov-report=term-missing

# Only unit tests (fast)
pytest tests/test_core_state_machine.py tests/test_llm_guardrails.py tests/test_core_humanizer.py tests/test_llm_engine.py -v

# Only integration tests
pytest tests/test_core_scheduler.py tests/test_bots_inbound_listener.py tests/test_services_conversation_service.py -v

# Security check
bandit -r app/ -ll --format txt

docs.title: Test Maintenance Policy
docs.text:
Tests added during Assignment 4 are maintained product assets. Later project work must: - Keep all existing tests passing after code changes, or - Replace removed tests with documented equivalent or stronger coverage when the product feature changes.

Disabling or deleting tests without replacement is not permitted unless the tested module is also removed.

docs.title: User Acceptance Tests
docs.text:
============================================================================= UAT 1: Verify 24/7 Production Availability via VPS Deployment ============================================================================= Description: Ensures the MVP v2 is successfully deployed to the production VPS and can reliably handle user interactions at any time, independently of a local development environment.

Acceptance Criteria: - GIVEN the AI assistant application is fully deployed to the production VPS - WHEN a user sends a message to the assistant via the messaging interface outside of standard business hours (e.g., at 3:00 AM) - THEN the system should successfully receive and process the request - AND the AI assistant should reply with a relevant response within the defined latency threshold (e.g., < 3 seconds) - AND no server timeouts, 502 Bad Gateway, or "application sleeping" errors should occur.

============================================================================= UAT 2: Verify Natural Conversational Flow and Structured Lead Nurturing ============================================================================= Description: Ensures the enhanced system prompts successfully guide the AI to build trust through conversation rather than immediately pushing a hard sale or funnel link to the user.

Acceptance Criteria: - GIVEN a new, unrecognized user initiates a chat with the AI assistant - WHEN the user asks top-of-funnel questions (e.g., asking for general information or expressing a common pain point) - THEN the AI assistant should respond using a natural, empathetic tone that directly addresses the user's specific question - AND the assistant must NOT output a direct purchase link or aggressive sales call-to-action in its initial replies - AND ONLY AFTER a predefined nurturing condition is met (e.g., 3 successful value-adding exchanges or the user explicitly asking for pricing/next steps) - THEN the assistant should smoothly transition the user into the structured sales funnel. =============================================================================

docs.title: User Story Index
docs.text:
This file is the authoritative registry of stable user-story IDs and current membership. Detailed mutable content lives in the linked issues.

ID Short title MoSCoW priority Issue Requirement status Work Status Sprint US-01 Getting product information Must Have #3 Active To Do Sprint 1 US-02 Contact Product Owner Must Have #4 Active To Do Sprint 1 US-03 Bot Setup and Funnel Upload Must Have #5 Active In Progress Sprint 1 US-04 Labor Cost Reduction Must Have #6 Active To Do Sprint 1 US-05 Human Manager Escalation Should Have #7 Active To Do — US-06 Increase Lead Turnover Should Have #8 Active To Do — US-07 24/7 Availability Should Have #9 Active In Progress Sprint 1 US-08 LLM Provider Selection Should Have #10 Active Done Sprint 1 US-09 Manual Dialog Takeover Could Have #11 Active To Do — US-010 Telegram Admin Bot for Management Could Have #12 Active In Progress Sprint 1

docs.title: Removed User Stories
docs.text: No stories have been removed at this time.

docs.title: Historical Context
docs.text: • Historical Assignment 2 artifact: reports/week2/user-stories.md

docs.title: Traceability Notes
docs.text:
• Stable IDs (US-xx) are preserved from Assignment 2.
  • Work Status mirrors the current issue work status.
  • Issues link to the live GitHub tracker maintained by the team.

docs.title: Architecture Documentation
docs.text: This document is the maintained architecture overview for AI Sales Manager — an autonomous Telegram outbound sales system with LLM-generated dialogues, campaign scheduling, and lead funnel management.

docs.title: Architectural Views
docs.text: View Diagram source Purpose Static view static-view/component-diagram.puml Components, external systems, and communication paths Dynamic view dynamic-view/inbound-reply-sequence.puml Runtime flows (sequence diagrams) for important workflows Deployment view deployment-view/deployment-diagram.puml Runtime deployment, datastores, and customer access path

docs.title: Component Diagram (Static View)
docs.text: Diagram source: static-view/component-diagram.puml

docs.title: What the diagram shows
docs.text:
The component diagram groups the product into four layers:

  1. External actors and platforms — Sales Director/Operator, Telegram leads, Telegram MTProto network, and LLM provider APIs (OpenRouter / DashScope).
  2. Application components — Admin Bot, REST API, scheduler, inbound listener, SellerClient (Pyrogram), LLM engine, guardrails, state machine, humanizer, intent classifier, funnel manager, and supporting services.
  3. Data stores — PostgreSQL (primary persistence and APScheduler job store) and Redis (conversation cache invalidation).
  4. Communication paths — outbound campaign processing (scheduler path), inbound reply handling (listener path), and operator management (Admin Bot and REST API both use shared services directly).

Important protocols: MTProto (Pyrogram user sessions), HTTPS (LLM APIs), async SQLAlchemy (PostgreSQL), in-process calls between core modules.

docs.title: Coupling and cohesion
docs.text:
Cohesion is high within bounded packages: app/llm/ (generation, guardrails, prompts), app/core/ (scheduler, state machine, humanizer), app/bots/ (Telegram integration), app/services/ (persistence, notifications).

Coupling is lowest at the guardrails boundary (ADR-001, ADR-004): every outbound and inbound message passes through apply_guardrails() before Pyrogram dispatch. The state machine (ADR-002) is a pure module with no I/O, consumed by scheduler and inbound handler alike.

Admin Bot and REST API are sibling entry points — both call shared services and the scheduler directly; the bot does not route through the HTTP API.

docs.title: Maintainability implications
docs.text:
• New funnel stages require explicit state-machine transitions and tests — predictable but not configurable at runtime.
  • LLM provider changes stay isolated in app/llm/engine.py.
  • Scheduler logic (ADR-003) centralises anti-spam and working-hours rules.
  • Trade-off: monolithic api container simplifies VPS deployment but couples scheduler restarts with inbound listeners.

docs.title: Quality requirements supported or constrained
docs.text: QR Effect of current structure QR-01 Guardrails component is the single pre-send gate QR-02 Isolated state machine; terminal states enforced centrally QR-03 Scheduler owns bounded cycle; LLM latency affects whole pipeline QR-04 Anti-repetition inside guardrails on both inbound and outbound paths

docs.title: Sequence Diagram (Dynamic View)
docs.text: Diagram source: dynamic-view/inbound-reply-sequence.puml

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A lead replies to an outbound campaign message in Telegram. The system receives the message via Pyrogram, matches the sender to a campaign conversation, classifies intent, updates state and funnel stage, generates an LLM reply with guardrails, applies human-like delays, and sends the response.

docs.title: Why this scenario is important
docs.text: This is the core MVP v2 conversational path (improved prompts, natural pacing, structured lead nurturing). It crosses five integration boundaries — Telegram, PostgreSQL, LLM APIs, guardrails, notifications — and failures are immediately visible to leads and operators.

docs.title: Architecture decisions, boundaries, and quality requirements
docs.text: Step ADR / QR Intent → state transition ADR-002, QR-02 Funnel stage advancement Multi-stage nurturing (hook → qualification → value → CTA) LLM cascade fallback Latency and resilience (QR-03) apply_guardrails() with retry ADR-001, QR-01 Anti-repetition (last 5 messages) ADR-004, QR-04 Humanizer delays Natural dialogue pacing (MVP v2 product goal) Hot-lead notification Operator handover boundary

docs.title: What the diagram shows
docs.text: The sequence follows one inbound message from Telegram delivery through persistence, intent-driven state update, LLM generation with guardrail retry, humanized send, and optional operator notification — involving Inbound Listener, Conversation Service, State Machine, LLM Engine, Guardrails, Humanizer, SellerClient, and Notification Service.

docs.title: Deployment Diagram (Deployment View)
docs.text: Diagram source: deployment-view/deployment-diagram.puml

docs.title: What the diagram shows
docs.text:
Docker Compose stack on a production VPS (Sprint 3 target) or local host:

Node Role api container FastAPI :8000, Admin Bot, APScheduler, Pyrogram sessions, static promo site postgres container PostgreSQL 15 — app data + APScheduler job store redis container Redis 7 — cache External Telegram (MTProto + Bot API), LLM APIs (HTTPS), GitHub Pages (docs only)

Customer-facing access: Telegram Admin Bot, MTProto lead sessions, HTTP :8000 (health/REST/landing page).

docs.title: Why this deployment model was chosen
docs.text: Single-VPS Docker Compose matches MVP v2 goals (24/7 availability) without Kubernetes overhead. All runtime components share PostgreSQL state and start together (ADR-003). The same docker-compose.yml used locally deploys to production with environment-specific secrets.

docs.title: How deployment supports or constrains the product
docs.text:
Supports: restart: unless-stopped, dependency health checks, env-based secrets, co-located scheduler job store.

Constrains: single-process monolith — deploy restarts all subsystems; no horizontal scaling without shared session management; LLM/Telegram latency depends on external networks.

docs.title: Operational considerations for the customer
docs.text:
1. Provide secrets via .env (Telegram API, LLM keys, bot token, encryption key).
  2. Run alembic upgrade head on first deploy and after schema changes.
  3. Monitor /health (degraded when scheduler is down).
  4. Back up PostgreSQL volume (postgres_data).
  5. Hosted MkDocs docs deploy separately via CI — not part of the runtime VPS stack.

docs.title: Architecture Decision Records
docs.text:
Important design choices are captured as ADRs in adr/. Each ADR documents context, the adopted decision, consequences, and linked quality requirements.

ADR Title Status Quality requirements ADR-001 LLM Output Guardrails Accepted QR-01 ADR-002 Deterministic Conversation State Machine Accepted QR-02 ADR-003 Scheduler-Driven Outbound Processing Accepted QR-03 ADR-004 Anti-Repetition Check for Generated Messages Accepted QR-04

docs.title: How the Architecture and Decisions Fit Together
Static view → structure
docs.text: The static view shows what the system is made of: internal modules (scheduler, state_machine, guardrails, llm/engine, SellerClient) and externals (Telegram, OpenRouter/DashScope, PostgreSQL, Redis). Low coupling between guardrails, state machine, and scheduler keeps each concern testable in isolation — which directly supports the Testability and Modifiability goals behind QR-01–QR-04.

docs.title: Dynamic view → behaviour
docs.text: The dynamic view traces how a request flows — typically the outbound path: scheduler tick → load campaign contacts → select account → LLM generate → guardrails → Pyrogram send → update state. This flow crosses the boundaries defined in ADR-001 (pre-send safety), ADR-003 (when and how sends happen), ADR-002 (state updates after replies), and ADR-004 (repetition rejection inside guardrails).

docs.title: Deployment view → operations
docs.text: The deployment view shows where code runs: a single application container hosts FastAPI, APScheduler jobs, and Pyrogram sessions; PostgreSQL holds persistent state and scheduler jobs; Redis supports caching. Customer access is through the Telegram Admin Bot and deployed Docker stack. Operational constraints (timezone, cooldown recovery, daily counter reset) are implemented inside ADR-003's scheduler jobs.

docs.title: ADRs → rationale and quality traceability
docs.text:
Each ADR explains why a specific pattern was chosen and which measurable quality requirement it satisfies:

  • ADR-001 + ADR-004 form the pre-dispatch safety gate for all LLM output — confidentiality and user-error protection before any Telegram send.
  • ADR-002 ensures funnel integrity — terminal states stop further messaging and keep analytics trustworthy.
  • ADR-003 provides timed, recoverable outbound processing — the 5-minute cycle, persistent job store, and account rotation enforce performance and availability constraints.

Bidirectional traceability is maintained:

  • Each ADR links to its quality requirement(s) in docs/quality-requirements.md.
  • Each quality requirement links back to its ADR and automated test in docs/quality-requirement-tests.md.

When product scope, deployment, or quality targets change, update the relevant view diagram and the affected ADR (or add a new ADR that supersedes the old one) rather than silently changing implementation.

docs.title: Quality Requirements and Architecture
docs.text:
Full quality requirement definitions: docs/quality-requirements.md

Automated verification mapping: docs/quality-requirement-tests.md

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Development process and git workflow: docs/development-process.md
  • Testing strategy: docs/testing.md
  • Definition of Done: docs/definition-of-done.md

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
AI Sales Manager sends outbound Telegram messages through live user accounts (Pyrogram MTProto), not the Bot API. The LLM layer (app/llm/engine.py) generates message text on behalf of sales personas. Generated text can contain:

  • Off-brand or legally sensitive topics (politics, religion, hate speech)
  • Formatting that looks automated (markdown, emoji)
  • Script or language mismatches (CJK, Arabic characters in Russian sales dialogs)
  • Self-identification as a bot or AI assistant

If such content reaches SellerClient.send_message(), it can damage the client's brand, expose legal liability, or trigger Telegram account restrictions. The guardrail check must run before dispatch and must be deterministic enough to verify in automated tests.

Alternatives considered:

  1. Prompt-only constraints — cheaper to maintain, but LLMs still violate instructions unpredictably.
  2. Post-send moderation — too late; the message is already delivered.
  3. Human review for every message — does not scale for campaign throughput.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Introduce a dedicated guardrails module (app/llm/guardrails.py) that evaluates every LLM output through evaluate_guardrails() / apply_guardrails() before sending. The module checks:

  • Message length (max 300 words)
  • Forbidden topic keywords
  • Bot/AI self-identification phrases (whole-word matching)
  • Markdown symbols (#, *, _, `)
  • Emoji characters
  • CJK and Arabic script ranges

When any check fails, apply_guardrails() returns None. The LLM engine retries generation with a stricter prompt; if guardrails fail again, a safe fallback text is used instead of sending unsafe content.

The guardrails layer sits between the LLM engine and the outbound message pipeline (scheduler / inbound handler), keeping Telegram dispatch free of policy-violating text.

docs.title: Consequences and Tradeoffs
docs.text:
Positive:

  • Unsafe or off-brand content is blocked before it reaches Telegram, directly supporting QR-01.
  • Checks are pure functions with no I/O — fast, deterministic, and easy to unit-test (QRT-01).
  • Violation types are logged via GuardrailsResult.violations, which helps tune thresholds without changing dispatch code.

Negative:

  • Keyword-based topic blocking can reject legitimate messages that mention a forbidden word in an acceptable context.
  • Strict no-emoji and no-markdown rules may reduce conversational naturalness.
  • Guardrail rejection triggers LLM retries, adding latency and API cost on the outbound critical path (ADR-003).

Neutral:

  • Anti-repetition is implemented in the same module but documented separately in ADR-004 because it addresses a distinct quality concern (QR-04).

docs.title: Quality Requirements Addressed
docs.text: • QR-01: LLM Response Safety — Confidentiality (Security): blocks forbidden content before delivery.

docs.title: Related Architecture Views
docs.text:
• Static view: guardrails module between LLM Engine and Message Dispatcher components.
  • Dynamic view: outbound campaign flow — LLM generate → apply_guardrails() → send or retry.
  • Deployment view: guardrails run in-process inside the application container; no separate moderation service.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The sales funnel tracks each lead through stages such as cold, warm, hot, follow_up, objection_handler, meeting_booked, and closed. Campaign scheduling, follow-up timing, hot-lead alerts, and analytics all depend on the current conversation state.

Without explicit transition rules, state updates could be scattered across handlers (inbound bot, scheduler, admin API), leading to:

  • Closed or booked leads receiving further outbound messages
  • Incorrect funnel metrics (e.g., counting a meeting_booked lead as still warm)
  • Hard-to-reason-about edge cases when multiple events arrive close together

Alternatives considered:

  1. Free-form status strings updated ad hoc — flexible initially, but error-prone as features grow.
  2. Database triggers — centralizes rules but hides logic from application tests and code review.
  3. External workflow engine — adds operational complexity for a bounded set of states.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Implement a deterministic, table-driven state machine in app/core/state_machine.py:

  • States and events are typed literals (State, Event).
  • All allowed transitions live in _TRANSITIONS: dict[State, dict[Event, State]].
  • transition(current_state, event) returns the next state; undefined events leave the state unchanged.
  • Terminal states (meeting_booked, closed) have empty transition maps — they never exit.
  • is_terminal(state) is used by schedulers and handlers to skip further outbound messaging.

Inbound handlers classify user replies into events (positive_reply, negative_reply, meeting_intent, etc.) and call transition() once per event. The state machine module has no database or network dependencies.

docs.title: Consequences and Tradeoffs
docs.text:
Positive:

  • Transition behaviour is fully specified in one module, supporting QR-02 fault tolerance.
  • Terminal-state immutability prevents messaging closed or booked leads — a common source of customer-facing defects.
  • Pure functions enable exhaustive unit tests with 100% branch coverage (QRT-02).
  • New states require an explicit row in _TRANSITIONS, making funnel changes reviewable in PRs.

Negative:

  • Every new funnel stage or event type requires updating the transition table update plus tests — no runtime configuration.
  • informational events preserve state, which is correct but can surprise developers expecting a visible state change.
  • The state machine does not persist history; audit trails rely on conversation records elsewhere.

Neutral:

  • Funnel stage progression (app/core/funnel.py) complements but does not replace conversation state — both must stay aligned when the product evolves.

docs.title: Quality Requirements Addressed
docs.text: • QR-02: Conversation State Correctness — Fault Tolerance (Reliability): deterministic transitions and immutable terminal states.

docs.title: Related Architecture Views
docs.text:
• Static view: State Machine component consumed by Inbound Handler, Scheduler, and Analytics.
  • Dynamic view: inbound reply flow — classify event → transition() → persist state → conditional outbound.
  • Deployment view: runs in-process; state persisted in PostgreSQL via the ORM layer.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Outbound campaigns must send initial messages and follow-ups to many contacts across multiple Telegram accounts while respecting:

  • Working hours and timezone per script
  • Follow-up delay (default 24 h) and auto-close (48 h)
  • Per-account daily message limits and rate limits (1 message / 30 s)
  • Account cooldown after FloodWait / PeerFlood errors

A purely event-driven approach (send only when a user starts a campaign) would miss timed follow-ups and recovery after restarts. A tight loop or cron per campaign would not scale and would complicate persistence across deploys.

The scheduler interval is 5 minutes (QR-03). Processing must finish within that window for typical campaign sizes so jobs do not stack and duplicate sends.

Alternatives considered:

  1. Message queue workers (Celery/RQ) — better for very high throughput, but adds Redis queue semantics and worker processes beyond current MVP scope.
  2. Per-campaign asyncio tasks — hard to persist and recover after process restart.
  3. Manual operator-triggered sends only — unacceptable for autonomous follow-up and auto-close requirements.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Use APScheduler (AsyncIOScheduler) with a SQLAlchemyJobStore backed by PostgreSQL so job definitions survive application restarts.

Core design in app/core/scheduler.py:

  • process_campaigns() runs every 5 minutes via IntervalTrigger(minutes=5).
  • Only campaigns with status == "running" are processed.
  • For each pending contact, the scheduler checks working hours, follow-up delay, account eligibility (ready/active, valid session, cooldown expired), and daily limits.
  • Account selection rotates across eligible accounts; LLM generation and Pyrogram dispatch run in the same async job.
  • Supporting jobs: reset_daily_counters (cron midnight Europe/Moscow), recover_cooldown_accounts and auto_close_conversations (every 6 hours).
  • Campaign start via Admin Bot or API can trigger an immediate process_campaigns() run in addition to the periodic tick.

FloodWait and PeerFlood errors move accounts to cooldown rather than failing the entire batch.

docs.title: Consequences and Tradeoffs
docs.text:
Positive:

  • Bounded 5-minute processing cycle aligns with QR-03 time-behaviour scenarios.
  • SQLAlchemyJobStore gives crash-safe scheduling without a separate job broker.
  • Centralized process_campaigns() keeps anti-spam, working-hours, and account-rotation logic in one place — easier to test (QRT-03).
  • Immediate processing on campaign start improves perceived responsiveness while the periodic job handles follow-ups.

Negative:

  • Worst-case latency for a new pending contact is up to one scheduler interval (~5 min) unless manually triggered.
  • Heavy LLM latency for large batches can approach the interval boundary; guardrail retries (ADR-001) add further delay.
  • APScheduler requires a synchronous DB URL for the job store while the app uses async SQLAlchemy elsewhere — an intentional dual-driver compromise.

Neutral:

  • Scheduler correctness depends on accurate server time and timezone configuration in scripts and Docker environment.

docs.title: Quality Requirements Addressed
docs.text: • QR-03: Outbound Message Delivery Performance — Time Behaviour (Performance Efficiency): bounded scheduler cycle for account selection, generation, and dispatch.

docs.title: Related Architecture Views
docs.text:
• Static view: Scheduler orchestrates Campaign Service, Account Pool, LLM Engine, Guardrails, and SellerClient (Pyrogram).
  • Dynamic view: primary outbound sequence diagram — scheduler tick through message delivery.
  • Deployment view: scheduler runs inside the main application container; job state stored in PostgreSQL; Redis used for caching elsewhere in the stack.

docs.title: ADR-004: Anti-Repetition Check for Generated Messages
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
AI Sales Manager may generate several messages within the same conversation (initial outreach, follow-up, objection handling). LLMs tend to reuse phrasing, especially with similar prompts and short context windows. Sending the same or near-identical text twice to a lead:

  • Signals automation and reduces trust
  • Increases the risk of Telegram spam filters flagging the account
  • Violates the product goal of human-like, varied sales dialogues

Customer feedback on MVP v1 highlighted that repetitive outbound text makes conversations feel robotic. A repetition check must run automatically on every generated message before send, without operator intervention.

Alternatives considered:

  1. Higher LLM temperature only — reduces but does not eliminate repetition; non-deterministic and hard to test.
  2. Embedding-based similarity — more semantically accurate, but adds model/API dependency and latency on every check.
  3. Store-and-compare last N messages with string similarity — lightweight, deterministic, testable.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Implement anti-repetition in app/llm/guardrails.py as check_anti_repetition():

  • Compare the candidate message against the last 5 outbound messages in the same conversation.
  • Use Python's difflib.SequenceMatcher ratio as the similarity metric.
  • Reject messages with similarity ≥ 0.8 (configurable threshold parameter).
  • Integrate into evaluate_guardrails() — a repetition violation causes apply_guardrails() to return None, triggering LLM retry with stricter instructions (same pipeline as ADR-001).

The last-message list is supplied by the caller (scheduler or inbound handler) from conversation history already loaded for context.

docs.title: Consequences and Tradeoffs
docs.text:
Positive:

  • Repetitive outputs are blocked before Telegram delivery, supporting QR-04 user error protection.
  • SequenceMatcher is fast, has no external dependencies, and supports boundary-value unit tests at the 0.8 threshold (QRT-04).
  • Lives alongside other guardrails checks, so one call site enforces all pre-send policies.

Negative:

  • Lexical similarity ≠ semantic similarity — paraphrased repeats with different wording may pass; structurally different messages on the same topic may fail.
  • The 0.8 threshold is a heuristic; legitimate follow-ups that restate a call-to-action may be rejected and require LLM retry.
  • Comparing only the last 5 messages does not detect repetition against older history in long threads.

Neutral:

  • Tuning the threshold or window size requires updating tests and possibly revisiting this ADR if product feedback demands it.

docs.title: Quality Requirements Addressed
docs.text: • QR-04: Anti-Repetition in Generated Messages — User Error Protection (Usability): blocks near-duplicate outbound text before send.

docs.title: Related Architecture Views
docs.text:
• Static view: anti-repetition is a sub-responsibility of the Guardrails component (ADR-001).
  • Dynamic view: LLM generate → guardrails (including repetition check) → send or retry loop.
  • Deployment view: in-process check; conversation history read from PostgreSQL before each generation.

docs.title: ADR-005
docs.text: Note: This ADR is a maintained architecture decision record for MVP v2.

docs.title: ADR-005: External Prompt Configuration and Versioning
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: LLM prompts are a high-churn part of the product. Customer feedback on MVP v1 showed that the first messages felt too salesy, and the team expects frequent A/B testing of prompt wording. Embedding prompts directly in app/llm/prompts.py forces a code change, review, and redeploy for every wording tweak and mixes prompt content with orchestration logic.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Move all prompt templates, language/emoji rules, nurturing rules, and intent-classification prompt into an external JSON file (app/config/prompts/v1.json). Load the configuration at startup via a cached loader (app.config.prompts.load_prompt_config) and use safe template formatting so missing placeholders do not crash the application.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: prompt changes become configuration changes; version is tracked in version field; faster iteration and safer rollback.
  • Positive: prompt content is reviewable as structured data rather than Python f-strings.
  • Negative: startup depends on the config file being present; a missing or invalid file raises an error early (this is acceptable because the failure is obvious).

docs.title: Related Quality Requirements
docs.text: • QR-05: Prompt Configuration Maintainability

docs.title: ADR-006
docs.text: Note: This ADR is a maintained architecture decision record for MVP v2.

docs.title: ADR-006: Funnel Upload and Preview API
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: MVP v1 supported funnel configuration only through the Script model and Admin Bot. Customer feedback requested the ability to upload funnel definitions from files or markdown. The team also needs a safe way to preview a funnel before attaching it to a campaign.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Introduce a dedicated Funnel entity with:

  • app/models/funnel.py — persistence model.
  • app/schemas/funnel.py — request/response schemas.
  • app/services/funnel_parser.py — parsers for json and text formats with validation (stages list, unique names, required fields).
  • app/api/funnels.py — POST /api/funnels/preview (non-persistent) and POST /api/funnels/upload (persistent, with overwrite guard for running campaigns).

The parser normalizes every stage to a common schema so the rest of the system can consume custom funnels the same way as default funnels.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: operators can version and share funnel definitions outside the application.
  • Positive: preview endpoint prevents broken funnels from reaching running campaigns.
  • Negative: adds a new table and migration; running-campaign overwrite guard requires explicit force=true.

docs.title: Related Quality Requirements
docs.text: • QR-06: Funnel Definition Validity

docs.title: ADR-007
docs.text: Note: This ADR is a maintained architecture decision record for MVP v2.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: After MVP v1 deployment, the team had no structured way to know whether the API, scheduler, PostgreSQL, or Redis were healthy. Container restarts were manual, and logs were inconsistent. MVP v2 must be production-ready enough to self-report health and survive routine failures.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
1. Keep the existing /health endpoint (checks DB + scheduler) as the primary uptime signal.
  2. Add centralized logging via app/logging_config.py that writes structured plain-text logs to stderr, controlled by the LOG_LEVEL environment variable.
  3. Add a Docker Compose healthcheck to the api service and set restart: unless-stopped for postgres, redis, and api.

The health endpoint is intentionally lightweight so it can be polled every 30 seconds by UptimeRobot or similar services.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: failures are visible through standard container logs and uptime checks.
  • Positive: Docker restarts unhealthy/failed containers automatically.
  • Negative: the health endpoint does not verify Telegram or LLM provider connectivity; those are checked indirectly through scheduler logs and outbound metrics.

docs.title: Related Quality Requirements
docs.text: • QR-07: Production Health Observability

docs.title: ADR-008
docs.text: Note: This ADR is a maintained architecture decision record for MVP v2.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text: The customer and team need a quantitative answer to “How many dialogs are handled by the AI without human intervention?” This metric guides prioritization of automation improvements and identifies when operator handoff is too frequent.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Add a boolean column was_escalated to the conversations table. Any operator-driven status change through PUT /conversations/{id}/status sets was_escalated = true. Provide GET /analytics/automation-rate that returns total conversations, AI-handled count, escalated count, and percentage.

This definition intentionally treats any human operator interaction as escalation, which is conservative and easy to explain to stakeholders.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Positive: simple, accurate metric derived directly from the existing conversation table.
  • Positive: no additional tracking service required; works with current analytics endpoint.
  • Negative: future manual takeovers that do not use the status endpoint would not be captured; if such a feature is added, it must also set was_escalated.

docs.title: Related Quality Requirements
docs.text: • QR-08: AI Automation Rate Accuracy
